root.label: Cell Morphology
root.description: After confirming Gram negative stain (pink/red), recall the shape of your cells.
root.question: What is the cell morphology (shape) of your Gram negative organism?

oxidase_cocci.label: Oxidase Test
oxidase_cocci.description: Apply oxidase reagent (tetramethyl-p-phenylenediamine) to a colony on filter paper. Purple/blue color within 10 seconds = positive. Gram negative cocci are nearly all oxidase positive.
oxidase_cocci.question: What is the result of the oxidase test?

sugar_fermentation.label: Sugar Fermentation (CTA)
sugar_fermentation.description: Inoculate Cystine Tryptic Agar (CTA) tubes containing glucose and maltose. Yellow color (acid) = fermentation. Used to differentiate Neisseria species from each other and from Moraxella.
sugar_fermentation.question: Which sugars are fermented (acid production in CTA tubes)?

result_meningitidis.label: Neisseria meningitidis
result_meningitidis.description: Gram negative diplococcus, oxidase positive, ferments glucose and maltose. Leading cause of bacterial meningitis and meningococcemia. Carried asymptomatically in the nasopharynx of ~10% of people. Polysaccharide capsule is the key virulence factor. Vaccine-preventable.

result_gonorrhoeae.label: Neisseria gonorrhoeae
result_gonorrhoeae.description: Gram negative diplococcus, oxidase positive, ferments glucose only (not maltose). Causes gonorrhea, pelvic inflammatory disease, and neonatal ophthalmia. Requires enriched media (Thayer-Martin or chocolate agar) due to fastidious growth requirements. No vaccine available.

result_moraxella.label: Moraxella catarrhalis
result_moraxella.description: Gram negative diplococcus, oxidase positive, does not ferment glucose or maltose. DNase positive; "hockey puck" sign on blood agar (colonies slide intact). Normal upper respiratory flora; causes otitis media, sinusitis, and COPD exacerbations. Nearly all strains produce β-lactamase.

result_acinetobacter.label: Acinetobacter spp.
result_acinetobacter.description: Gram negative coccobacillus, oxidase negative (unlike most GN cocci). Ubiquitous in soil and water; major nosocomial pathogen in ICU settings. Notorious for acquiring multi-drug resistance (MDRAB). Causes ventilator-associated pneumonia, wound infections, and bacteremia.

oxidase_rods.label: Oxidase Test
oxidase_rods.description: Find the oxidase test in your lab manual. There are many ways to test for oxidase acivity, but we will use sterile swabs and Spottest oxidase reagent vials (also known as ampules). you will start the procedure at the "second period" since you will have already grown your bacteria on plates.
oxidase_rods.question: What is the result of the oxidase test?

lactose_gneg_oxidase_neg.label: Lactose Fermentation
lactose_gneg_oxidase_neg.description: The glucose fermentation test is PART of a series of tests labeled "Carbohydrates I: Fermentation and beta galactosidase activity" in you manual. We will use the Durham tube procedure.
lactose_gneg_oxidase_neg.question: Does the organism ferment lactose?

indole.label: Indole Test
indole.description: The indole test is one of the four IMViC tests, described in exercise 23 in your book. Read the procedure carefully to determine which reagents you need.
indole.question: Does your organism produce indole?

result_ecoli.label: Escherichia coli
result_ecoli.description: Escherichia coli is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole positive, and citrate negative.

h2s.label: hydrogen sulfide (H2S) test
h2s.description: The hydrogen sulfide test is one of the four IMViC tests, described in exercise 23 in your book. Read the procedure carefully to determine which reagents you need.
h2s.question: Is H₂S produced?

motility_enterobac_h2sneg.label: Motility Test
motility_enterobac_h2sneg.description: There are many ways to test for motility - consult with Dr. Hussa to find the best method.
motility_enterobac_h2sneg.question: Is the organism motile? Also, observe the color of the colonies on petri plates to determine if they are red.

glucose.label: Glucose fermentation Test
glucose.description: The glucose fermentation test is PART of a series of tests labeled "Carbohydrates I: Fermentation and beta galactosidase activity" in you manual. We will use the Durham tube procedure.
glucose.question: Does your organism ferment glucose?

salt_curved.label: Salt Tolerance + Cell Morphology
salt_curved.description: Ask Dr. Hussa. This isn't in your lab manual. Basically, we need to have growth media that is comprised of 6.5% NaCl. Regular growth medium has 1% NaCl. Cell curvature should have been visible during our previous microscope analysis.
salt_curved.question: Does the organism tolerate 6.5% NaCl and/or have curved cells?

options.label: No — straight rod, no salt tolerance

result_vibrio.label: Vibrio cholerae
result_vibrio.description: Gram negative curved rod, oxidase positive, glucose fermenter, salt tolerant. Causes cholera — profuse rice-water diarrhea from cholera toxin (ADP-ribosylates Gs → ↑cAMP → Cl⁻/H₂O secretion). Can lose 20 L/day. Transmitted via contaminated water. Positive "cholera red" reaction (string test positive). O1 and O139 serotypes cause epidemic cholera.

result_aeromonas.label: Aeromonas hydrophila
result_aeromonas.description: Gram negative straight rod, oxidase positive, glucose fermenter, not salt tolerant. Found in fresh and brackish water and soil. Causes gastroenteritis (from contaminated water/seafood), wound infections after water exposure, and septicemia in immunocompromised patients. Intrinsically resistant to ampicillin.

pyocyanin.label: Pyocyanin Pigment
pyocyanin.description: Observe culture for blue-green pigment (pyocyanin) on Mueller-Hinton or cetrimide agar. Pyocyanin is a phenazine pigment unique to P. aeruginosa among common clinical isolates. Also look for a grape-like or corn tortilla odor.
pyocyanin.question: Is blue-green (pyocyanin) pigment produced?

result_pseudomonas.label: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
result_pseudomonas.description: Gram negative rod, oxidase positive, non-fermenter (glucose oxidizer only), produces pyocyanin (blue-green) and pyoverdine (yellow-green). Characteristic grape-like odor. Major pathogen in cystic fibrosis (mucoid biofilm-forming strains), burn wounds, and healthcare-associated infections. Intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics; often requires anti-pseudomonal agents.

result_burkholderia.label: Burkholderia / Stenotrophomonas
result_burkholderia.description: Gram negative rod, oxidase positive (Burkholderia) or variable, non-fermenter, no pyocyanin. B. cepacia complex is a devastating pathogen in CF patients (accelerates lung decline, "cepacia syndrome"). S. maltophilia is an emerging opportunistic pathogen with very high intrinsic antibiotic resistance (resistant to carbapenems); TMP-SMX is drug of choice.

Citrate_test.label: Citrate test
Citrate_test.description: The citrate test is part of a group of tests called IMViC, described in exercise 23 of your book. Please read through the procedure to find the materials required for the citrate test.
Citrate_test.question: Can your organism use citrate as the sole carbon source?

VP_and_H2S.label: VP and Hydrogen sulfide tests
VP_and_H2S.description: The VP (Voges-Proskauer) and H2S (hydrogen sulfide) tests are two of the four IMViC tests, described in exercise 23 in your book. Read the procedure carefully to determine which reagents you need. Fill out one request form for each test, or make it clear you are requesting for two tests on one form so you get more points!
VP_and_H2S.question: What are the results of this combination of tests?

Citrobacter_diversus.label: Citrobacter diversus
Citrobacter_diversus.description: Citrobacter diversus is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole positive, citrate positive, and VP negative.

Erwinia_chrysanthemi.label: Erwinia chrysanthemi
Erwinia_chrysanthemi.description: Erwinia chrysanthemi is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole positive, citrate positive, VP positive, and H2S positive.

Klebsiella_oxytoca.label: Klebsiella oxytoca
Klebsiella_oxytoca.description: Klebsiella oxytoca is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole positive, citrate positive, VP positive, and H2S negative.

MR_VP.label: MR and VP tests
MR_VP.description: The MR (methyl red) and VP (Voges-Proskauer) are two of the four IMViC tests, described in exercise 23 in your book. Read the procedure carefully to determine which reagents you need.
MR_VP.question: What are the results of these two tests?

Enterobacter_intermedius.label: Enterobacter intermedius
Enterobacter_intermedius.description: Enterobacter intermedius is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole negative, MR positive, and VP positive.

Citrobacter_freundii.label: Citrobacter freundii
Citrobacter_freundii.description: Citrobacter freundii is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole negative, MR positive, VP negative, and H2S positive.

Serratia_fonticola.label: Serratia fonticola
Serratia_fonticola.description: Serratia fonticola is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole negative, MR positive, VP negative, H2S negative, and motile

Klebsiella_pneumoniae_subsp_ozaenae.label: Klebsiella pneumoniae subspecies ozaenae
Klebsiella_pneumoniae_subsp_ozaenae.description: Klebsiella pneumoniae subspecies ozaenae is a gram negative rod that is oxidase negative, lactose positive, indole negative, MR positive, VP negative, H2S negative, and nonmotile.

are_you_sure.label: Are you sure?
are_you_sure.description: If you land here, it is not because you've done something wrong, but these results are unusual. Consult with Dr. Hussa on how to proceed.
